top_methods: Structural equation modeling (SEM) based on Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)
top_tasks: Predicting pedestrian violating road-crossing behavior intentions
Evaluating contribution of TPB components and extended factors
Pedestrian street crossing under various mobile phone distraction conditions
top_datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China (2015)
narrative: The appendix records 2 papers and 10 matrix rows used to generate the review synthesis.

The most visible methodological families are Structural equation modeling (SEM) based on Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), Controlled laboratory experiment with eye-tracking, while recurring tasks include Predicting pedestrian violating road-crossing behavior intentions, Evaluating contribution of TPB components and extended factors, Pedestrian street crossing under various mobile phone distraction conditions.

evidence_table.title: Effects of mobile phone distraction on pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection: An outdoor experimental study
evidence_table.venue: Accident Analysis and Prevention, Volume 115, Pages 170-177
tasks: Pedestrian street crossing under various mobile phone distraction conditions
metrics: Initiate time (time between green light and crossing initiation)
claims: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrians' crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction, with music distraction having the least impact

Music distraction causes pedestrians to initiate crossing later (1.28s vs 0.69s), increase pupil diameter (2.86mm vs 2.75mm), and reduce scanning frequency (6.00 vs 6.82) and fixation points toward traffic signal areas

Phone conversation distraction causes pedestrians to cross at slower speeds (1.37m/s vs 1.46m/s), direct fewer fixation points to right traffic area, and spend less fixation time and average fixation duration on left traffic area

evidence_table.title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
methods: Structural equation modeling (SEM) based on Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)
tasks: Predicting pedestrian violating road-crossing behavior intentions
Evaluating contribution of TPB components and extended factors
datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China (2015)
metrics: RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation)
claims: Instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency are significant predictors of pedestrian violating crossing behavior intention, providing better predictive utility than the basic TPB model (Model 4 achieved best fit: CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030)
Descriptive norm is a stronger predictor than subjective norm

when descriptive norm was added, subjective norm became non-significant, indicating that pedestrians' observations of family and friends' behavior have greater influence than perceived approval from them
